data.text:
INT: Tape 10 of Mark Verstandig. Yes, please continue the story of this porter. 

MV: Yeah. Now, in the train, at dawn, and first of all, he had to do-- he-- he could doze off a little bit, but he had to keep an eye on all his parcels, parcels around him. Dawn, he put on his tallis and tefillin and said the prayers, in the train. This gives you an example, too, that people were tolerant. He was praying like he would be in the-- in the synagogue. 

He came to Cracow. And there he had to hire a cab, a horse and cab again for the whole day. And he came with my parcel of food to the-- to the-- to the [NON-ENGLISH], which was hostile for the Jewish university students. 

The parcel consisted of a loaf of bread, a round loaf of black bread as big as a-- as a cart wheel, [POINTS] and a loaf of butter, at least 2 kilo, and hard cheese. And all this, he schlepped up to the third floor, with his-- with his black kaftan and this black this. He was sweating. He waked us up. 

And later, he collects it. He delivered all the parcels in the town. And he-- then he collected all kinds of parcels, which he carried for them, for the merchants and shopkeepers in Mielec. 

Well, considering that you spent, back and forth, 10 hours in the train, back and forth, with an hour in the middle, because four hours, it was the direct travel, but there's another in the middle is-- it is 10 hours in the train and 10 hours in-- on the street in Cracow. 

He worked 20 hours out of 24 hours from Sunday to Thursday night, five days a week, 100 hours, in order that he live-- to-- to-- to have enough to live in two dark rooms, which never saw the sun, in preparation, because he had to work for the Shabbos. 

When it came Friday night, you wouldn't recognize him. He was a king on Friday night. Now this was the poor fellow. And the majority, the great majority of Jews hacked out a living the same way as this-- as-- as-- as this Birnbaum. 

Shops around-- next door where I lived was a-- a corner store. And when you walked in, you could see a barrel of herring and a board on top. And they were selling herring by the centimeter, a piece of herring a centimeter. And this is what people live. The-- the-- the majority of people, who-- who could-- who did not beg and who were not very wealthy, were lucky to have a little piece of herring with a piece of bread and cottage cheese before they went to bed. 

And that-- and the-- and the staple food were potatoes. Again, the same type of-- of the-- the-- the-- the same as in the village. Now that, more or less, gives you a-- a picture of the strata, of the strata of the Jewish community in the shtetl. And that was the same everywhere. 

There were a few families, very wealthy, landowners, no matter how much they were mortgaged. There were merchants who really had a lot of money. And there were other shopkeepers and so on. But the majority, the majority were people working the same way as this Birnbaum. And a large amount were just beggars. 

INT: What about the people that were professionals in the larger cities? 

MV: Ah, the professionals were the-- were a-- were a-- they were as a-- a really on a very high standard. I mean the doctors, the lawyers. They-- they-- they-- their standard of living was as high as-- as the standard of living of the rich merchants. Yes, there were-- there were such a one, too. 

And the same applies really-- similar situation was you could see in-- you could see in the villages. The peasant, the farmhands were the poorest people. But then there were farmers, small farmers, which had their own plot of land, let's say, two hectares, three hectares. They had meat every Sunday. More-- mostly they had the rabbits, the domestic rabbits, which they killed for-- for Sunday. And from time to time, they killed-- they-- they-- they-- they killed a pig. And that was really a feast for months to go. 

Now these were the smaller farmers. And there were larger farmers, who had-- who really had a-- made a good life. And then there were the landowners, the aristocracy, the counts, the Poniatowskis and the-- the-- and the Potowskis and so on and so on. There were-- there were vast-- they had vast forests-- forests and-- and land. And-- and they-- and most of the time, they spent overseas in Paris. 

No, none of the Jews went to Paris to make a-- I mean, to-- to-- just for luxury. If you went-- if a Jew went to Paris, it must have been for some kind of business, but not just to sit in Paris and to live there on the strength of the wealth, which they have in Poland. 

INT: To finish the-- the socioeconomic picture, would you say that, in the period of the 1920s, 1930s, Jews who were in the professions, would they be one in 20 or one in 50 out of Jews? 

MV: I don't know. I don't know. Let's say, it wouldn't be a one in 20. For instance, in Mielec, I could tell you. And I don't have the statistics. Really, there could have been about maybe 20 or 15 solicitors, Jews who made a good living, and a few Jewish doctors. These were the professionals. 

On the other hand, you take-- and if you take the Polish population-- the Polish population in town were mostly public servants, whether they were employed by the [NON-ENGLISH], which was the district administration, or by local government. And the local government had to-- had the local government from Mielec, a local government, let us say, for a smaller village around. And then there was [NON-ENGLISH], which means, again, a district, a-- a district office for local governments. 

And they employed people. They bought a lot of people. And they had a lot of, again, intelligentsia, whether they were vets or doctors or solicitors. And some of them were merchants, just the same merchants and exporters. Then there were rich people, and there were-- and there were poor people. There was-- it was-- it's-- there were these people who were-- who-- who were the poorest in town. Well, there were such people, too. 

But no, but more or less, it was a-- it was almost the same proportion of rich people and small people. But in the-- in the-- in the between, there was this mass of public servants, whether they were government or local government. And this did not exist with the Jewish community. In this respect, they were much better off than the Jewish community at large. 

INT: It didn't exist because of what reasons? Why didn't it exist in the Jewish community? Why weren't Jews public servants? 

MV: The Jewish weren't public servants because the public servant didn't accept him. In our town, in our town, there were quite a number of people, who-- who were professional teachers, finished the teachers' seminary. Oh, they had higher education, but they couldn't get a job. 

As a matter of fact, my wife's cousin, who died two years ago, at the age of 87, in London, her daughter came now back to Poland. And that's a different story. But she had-- she brought with her, her mother's diploma, a diploma from the-- the Cracow University. She was a-- a scholar of classics, of Greek classic, Greek and classic Latin, Latin and Greek. But she couldn't get a job. 

She couldn't get a teacher's job, not only in a high school, not as a tutor at a university, but she couldn't get a job even in a-- in a-- in a primary school. They were just walking the streets. You wouldn't believe it, but if there would have been a dole-- there was no dole in Poland-- 90% of Jews in Poland, whether it was in shtetl, in Mielec, or in Cracow, would have been unemployed, ready to accept a dole, which didn't exist. That was the situation. 

In larger-- in-- in larger towns, let us say, in Lodz, there was a Jewish proletariat. And how this looks like, how their-- how their lives look like, it's enough to read Bashevis, not-- not Bashevis, Bashevis Singer's brother, this older brother, yeah, the-- his-- his book about this-- this 'Family Ashkenazi.' They describe-- he describes of this proletariat that exists there. 

As a matter of fact, yesterday, a friend of ours, a poet, Jake-- Jacob Rosenberg, launched his book. And he reminded the audience-- it was a large audience, maybe-- maybe 300 or 400 people. He reminded the audience that he was one of five, a family, one of five. And they lived in one room in Lodz one room in Lodz, a family with five children. 

His father never in his life had a new pair of trousers or a new jumper or a new shirt or anything new. He didn't buy any new things, only secondhand. But his three children, three of his children, he sent to private schools. 

See? He works his life, everything, to send the three children to private schools. But he never had, in his life, a new shirt or a new pair of trousers. That was the life of the proletariat in big towns. 

INT: Getting back to the public service or public jobs in Poland, government jobs, was this an official policy or unofficial policy of not hiring Jewish people? 

MV: Yes, that was-- couldn't be official, because it would have been not sanctioned by the Constitution. But that was the real situation, which existed, the real situation. I've got here a friend, in-- in Melbourne. He was a school friend in the Mielec Gymnasium and at the university. 

Now, his father-- it's very interesting. His father was a public servant. His father was a-- a professional officer from the Austrian Army, a professional-- a professional officer. When Poland was established, they-- Poland committed itself to employ and accept all public servants, all members of the army, of the Austrian, of the Russian, of the Prussian, into their new life in Poland. 

So his father was a public servant, an officer. And he was an officer and a-- a member of the-- of the Mielec [NON-ENGLISH]. He was in charge of the military affairs of the district. And he was a Jew. Very interesting. 

But after a few years, he was dismissed. But he couldn't be dismissed officially. He was put-- he was sent to the reserves, a reserve officer. And this [NON-ENGLISH] came to my father, who was a-- who was head of the Jewish council, asking him that he should give Mr. Rota-- he should give him a job as secretary. 

And every-- everybody in town was proud before that. That the only public servant in town is a Jewish officer, a Jewish member of the army, who walked around in his uniform. But he was dismissed, after a few years, to the reserve. But he was very much liked, and, therefore, he intervened that he should be given a job as a secretary of the Jewish council. 

And in this respect, you could see this, that it was official, because the [INAUDIBLE], himself was very friendly with him. He didn't want to dismiss him. But it was from above. And this is what I am pointing out, that the antisemitism in Poland came from above. Until later, it settled down. But it was engineered from the-- from-- from the people who were very friendly. 

You see? And he was a-- he was with the Jewish-- with the Jewish council. And his son is here. He's a friend of mine. As a matter of fact, I'm going to ask him to say something about-- to-- to meet you. 

INT: Let us now turn to the period where you finished your university in the mid '30s. 

MV: Who? 

INT: And let us talk about your period after you finished university-- 

MV: Yeah. 

INT: --in Cracow. What did you do for a professional life? 

MV: I was a lawyer. I was employed as a lawyer. I was employed as a lawyer. 

INT: In what year was that? 

MV: Or the article-- I mean it is called, here, article clerk-- article. But in Poland, it was called concipient. I was employed by Dr. J. Yakov Salpeter, Who was a brother-in-law of Frieda, my wife. And he employed me and-- in town. I used to go to court. And I didn't-- I-- I didn't-- I-- I didn't met any kind of anti-Jewish feelings in court. 

INT: What year was that when you started your article? 

MV: Beg your pardon? 

INT: What year did you start your articles? 

MV: In 1936. In 1936 I started my articles. And I was going to court. I was going to go-- but later, and later-- I mean, in the last, almost last year, after Adam Koc came to power a year later. So they started to send to the towns-- they started to send to the towns young judges, recruited from this Falangists, from this new generation of play-- not [NON-ENGLISH] and myself, of fascists. 

And this, there were a couple of-- of judges like that. And they behaved like fascists, not only in relation to Jews, to Jewish lawyers, but in relation to-- to Polish lawyers, who were in Poland, at that time, in my town, who were used to relate to-- to a nice, democratic relationship with everybody, with-- with Jews and non-Jews. It didn't matter. They were quite different people. 

And I remember that he used to-- I remember, asked us, with this new-- with this new idea of the fascists, to punish people, to-- for-- for the smallest-- for-- for-- for the smallest felony with high, with-- with-- with many months of prison and so on. And he behaved very rashly, very-- very-- very vulgar in relation with-- vis-a-vis all lawyers. 

And I remember a case. I describe this case. I remember a case when I had a Polish-- a Polish client, defending a Polish client. Normally, I had civil cases because Dr. Salpeter had-- he was known. He was a brilliant lawyer. He was known as a civilist, the so-called civil cases. But from time to time, there were criminal cases. 

So when there was a petty fellow, a petty felony, and I remember, he-- the-- the procedure was in Poland not like here. A procedure was that in criminal cases, the judge first tried, first of all, to interrogate the man who sent-- who was in dock, on the dock. And after the judge finished, it is the prosecutor, it was the policeman. The prosecutor was a magistrate. He was a prosecutor. He had this-- had this right to re-interrogate. And this ended with the-- the defendant-- the-- the lawyer of the-- of the defendant. 

And I could see, when he was interrogating this fellow, he is-- was dictating. That was the procedure. He was dictating to the minute what the-- what the-- the defendant said. Ask a question, and the question was minuted. The answer was minuted. But the answer was like the judge dictated to the clerk. And I could see that he is falsifying the testimony of the defendant. 

So I stood up and I said to him, I'm sorry, Your Honor. But he didn't say that. He said that. And why did you say-- well, why did you-- why did you say to the minutes, that he-- what you said to the minutes was not correct. He didn't say it. And the judge just ignored me. 

Question after question, he falsified the record. And then I stood up, and I said to the judge, Your Honor, I'm going to disappoint you. I'm not going to do like all-- like many of my elderly colleagues did, to leave the client and walk out because of your antics. You are falsifying the records. And I'm going to make an application that you should be removed from this case, because you are falsifying this. 

And at this moment, the prisoner-- the-- the defendant was not imprisoned, you see? But he could have-- he-- he could have imprisoned him. At that moment, I stood up. I pushed out the client and I said, go home. You'll be a-- there will be another judge who will be presiding, who-- who will be conducting your case. And I pushed him out. And I walked out. 

The same day, the afternoon, I brought my case. I-- I made an-- I made an application that the judge would be removed because-- and disciplined, because he was falsifying the record. Brought it in. You know, a fact was that he sent two-- two well-known solicitors, senior solicitors, to me, begged me to have a meeting, that he will do everything now. He will exclude himself. But that I shouldn't ruin his career. 

But that point that-- that is pointed out, that the-- that in the last years, in 1938, '39, the public service was already infiltrated from above with Falangists and fascists. 

INT: What were the names of some of these new judges? 

MV: Some of these? 

INT: Judges, these new judges. 

MV: I don't remember the name of the new judges. But I remember the judges. I remember the judges who were really models for democracy. I remember-- I mentioned in my book Kosowski. I remembered in my little book, [NON-ENGLISH]. These were judges of high, very high principles, who-- who-- who were ready to risk their lives in order to uphold the law. 

INT: Did they experience a lot of difficulty in those last years? 

MV: I don't know last year. But after-- after the-- after the war, they were honored. What happened to them later, I don't know. I tried to make contact with them, but somehow I could not. I knew that, let's say, Kosowski became later a high-- not a high court, but an-- a judge of the appeal, in the-- in the Court of Appeal. 

INT: What about in the years just before the war, '38, '39, did they have problems? 

MV: No, no, no, no no. The-- the good judges were-- were really-- no, as I mentioned to you, there was antisemitism that came only from above. It settled down soon because of the-- it was the years of-- of-- of Hitler. Hitler brutalized the masses. It settled down. But up till that time, the masses were really indifferent, not only indifferent, they were friendly with the Jews. 

INT: Did these particular judges have problems with their superiors in government? 

MV: No. I-- no, no, they didn't have problems with their superiors. Yeah, because besides this, the judges, by and large, in Poland, they were-- they were like-- like here, were independent. The Poles, the judges were independent. So they didn't have anything, not at what I know of, because they were, during the war, they were still working during the war. They were not-- they were not mistreated, whatever. 

But what they were doing is, when a position was vacant, they sent in-- the government nominated-- nominated judges from the Falangists and from the antisemites and from the national-- from-- from the index and so on. 

INT: Thank you. Let's continue next tape.
